site.name: StatusCode.dev
site.tagline: The fast, complete HTTP status code reference for developers
site.author: StatusCode.dev
site.affiliate_blurb: Recommended: ship faster with managed hosting.

affiliates.label: Datadog APM — trace 5xx errors in prod

categories.2xx: Success – the request was successfully received, understood, and accepted
categories.3xx: Redirection – further action needs to be taken to complete the request
categories.4xx: Client Error – the request contains bad syntax or cannot be fulfilled
categories.5xx: Server Error – the server failed to fulfill a valid request

codes.name: Continue
codes.summary: The server has received the request headers and the client should proceed to send the request body.
codes.when: Sent in response to an Expect: 100-continue header so a client can check whether the server will accept a large request body before sending it.
codes.fix: Usually handled automatically by HTTP libraries. No action needed unless you are implementing a low-level client.

codes.name: Switching Protocols
codes.summary: The server is switching protocols as requested by the client via the Upgrade header.
codes.when: Most commonly seen when upgrading an HTTP connection to a WebSocket.
codes.fix: Ensure your client sends the correct Upgrade and Connection headers.

codes.name: Early Hints
codes.summary: Used to return some response headers before the final HTTP message, allowing the browser to preload resources.
codes.when: Sent before a 200 to let browsers start fetching CSS/JS via Link: rel=preload headers.
codes.fix: Configure your CDN or server (e.g. Cloudflare, nginx) to emit 103 Early Hints.

codes.name: OK
codes.summary: The request succeeded. The meaning of success depends on the HTTP method.
codes.when: The standard response for successful GET, PUT, or POST requests that return a body.
codes.fix: Nothing to fix – this is the response you want.

codes.name: Created
codes.summary: The request succeeded and a new resource was created as a result.
codes.when: Returned after a successful POST that creates a resource; should include a Location header pointing to the new resource.
codes.fix: Ensure your API returns a Location header and the created representation.

codes.name: Accepted
codes.summary: The request has been accepted for processing, but processing has not completed.
codes.when: Used for asynchronous jobs – the work happens in a background queue.
codes.fix: Provide a status endpoint so clients can poll for completion.

codes.name: No Content
codes.summary: The server successfully processed the request and is not returning any content.
codes.when: Common for DELETE requests or PUT updates where no body is needed.
codes.fix: Do not send a response body with a 204 – it must be empty.

codes.name: Partial Content
codes.summary: The server is delivering only part of the resource due to a Range header sent by the client.
codes.when: Used for resumable downloads and video/audio streaming (byte-range requests).
codes.fix: Ensure your server supports Accept-Ranges and returns correct Content-Range headers.

codes.name: Moved Permanently
codes.summary: The URL of the requested resource has been changed permanently. The new URL is given in the Location header.
codes.when: Use when a page or domain has permanently moved; passes SEO link equity to the new URL.
codes.fix: Update links to the new URL and confirm the Location header is correct.

codes.name: Found
codes.summary: The requested resource resides temporarily under a different URL.
codes.when: Temporary redirects – e.g. redirecting to a login page. Does not transfer SEO equity like a 301.
codes.fix: If the move is permanent, use 301 instead so search engines update their index.

codes.name: Not Modified
codes.summary: Indicates the resource has not been modified since the version specified by request headers.
codes.when: Returned for conditional GETs using If-None-Match (ETag) or If-Modified-Since.
codes.fix: Nothing to fix – this saves bandwidth via caching. Verify your ETag logic if content seems stale.

codes.name: Temporary Redirect
codes.summary: The request should be repeated with another URL but future requests should still use the original URL. The method must not change.
codes.when: Like 302 but guarantees the HTTP method (e.g. POST) is preserved on redirect.
codes.fix: Use 307 instead of 302 when you must preserve the request method and body.

codes.name: Permanent Redirect
codes.summary: The resource is now permanently located at another URL, and the method must not change.
codes.when: Like 301 but preserves the HTTP method and body. Used for permanent API endpoint moves.
codes.fix: Use for permanent moves of non-GET endpoints.

codes.name: Bad Request
codes.summary: The server cannot or will not process the request due to a client error (malformed syntax, invalid framing).
codes.when: Triggered by malformed JSON, invalid query parameters, or bad request framing.
codes.fix: Validate the request payload and parameters on the client. Check for malformed JSON.

codes.name: Unauthorized
codes.summary: The client must authenticate itself to get the requested response.
codes.when: Missing or invalid authentication credentials (token, API key, session).
codes.fix: Send valid credentials via the Authorization header. Check token expiry.

codes.name: Forbidden
codes.summary: The client does not have access rights to the content; the server is refusing to authorize it.
codes.when: The user is authenticated but lacks permission, or a WAF/firewall blocked the request.
codes.fix: Verify the user's permissions/roles. Check firewall, CORS, or signed-URL rules.

codes.name: Not Found
codes.summary: The server cannot find the requested resource. The URL is not recognized.
codes.when: The most common error – a broken link, mistyped URL, or deleted resource.
codes.fix: Check the URL for typos, fix broken internal links, and add 301 redirects for moved pages.

codes.name: Method Not Allowed
codes.summary: The request method is known by the server but is not supported by the target resource.
codes.when: e.g. sending POST to an endpoint that only accepts GET.
codes.fix: Check the allowed methods in the Allow header and use the correct HTTP verb.

codes.name: Request Timeout
codes.summary: The server timed out waiting for the request.
codes.when: The client was too slow sending the full request.
codes.fix: Retry the request. Improve client connection speed or increase server keep-alive timeout.

codes.name: Conflict
codes.summary: The request conflicts with the current state of the server.
codes.when: Common in concurrent edits, duplicate creates, or version mismatches (optimistic locking).
codes.fix: Fetch the latest state, resolve the conflict, and retry the request.

codes.name: Gone
codes.summary: The requested content has been permanently deleted, with no forwarding address.
codes.when: Use instead of 404 when you intentionally removed a resource and want search engines to drop it fast.
codes.fix: If the resource moved, use 301 instead. Otherwise 410 is correct.

codes.name: Payload Too Large
codes.summary: The request entity is larger than limits defined by the server.
codes.when: Uploading a file or body bigger than the server/proxy allows.
codes.fix: Increase client_max_body_size (nginx) or the relevant body limit, or compress/chunk the upload.

codes.name: Unsupported Media Type
codes.summary: The media format of the requested data is not supported by the server.
codes.when: Sending the wrong Content-Type (e.g. text/plain to a JSON-only API).
codes.fix: Set the correct Content-Type header (e.g. application/json).

codes.name: I'm a teapot
codes.summary: The server refuses to brew coffee because it is, permanently, a teapot.
codes.when: An April Fools' joke from RFC 2324, sometimes used by APIs as an easter egg or to reject bots.
codes.fix: Nothing to fix – this is a joke status code. If unexpected, check the upstream service.

codes.name: Unprocessable Entity
codes.summary: The request was well-formed but contains semantic errors that prevent processing.
codes.when: Common in REST APIs for validation failures (e.g. email already taken).
codes.fix: Read the validation error details in the response body and correct the input.

codes.name: Too Many Requests
codes.summary: The user has sent too many requests in a given amount of time (rate limiting).
codes.when: You hit an API rate limit. A Retry-After header often indicates how long to wait.
codes.fix: Back off and retry after the Retry-After interval. Implement exponential backoff and caching.

codes.name: Unavailable For Legal Reasons
codes.summary: The resource is unavailable due to legal demands (censorship, DMCA, GDPR).
codes.when: Content blocked by court order or government request.
codes.fix: Nothing technical to fix – access is restricted for legal reasons.

codes.name: Internal Server Error
codes.summary: The server encountered an unexpected condition that prevented it from fulfilling the request.
codes.when: A generic catch-all for unhandled server-side exceptions.
codes.fix: Check server logs and stack traces. Add error handling for the failing code path.

codes.name: Not Implemented
codes.summary: The request method is not supported by the server and cannot be handled.
codes.when: The server does not recognize the request method (e.g. an unsupported verb).
codes.fix: Use a supported HTTP method or implement the missing handler.

codes.name: Bad Gateway
codes.summary: The server, acting as a gateway or proxy, received an invalid response from the upstream server.
codes.when: Common with reverse proxies (nginx, load balancers) when the backend is down or returns garbage.
codes.fix: Check that the upstream/backend service is healthy and reachable from the proxy.

codes.name: Service Unavailable
codes.summary: The server is not ready to handle the request, often due to maintenance or overload.
codes.when: The server is overloaded or down for maintenance. A Retry-After header may be present.
codes.fix: Scale up capacity, finish maintenance, or retry after the Retry-After interval.

codes.name: Gateway Timeout
codes.summary: The server, acting as a gateway, did not get a response in time from the upstream server.
codes.when: A proxy timed out waiting for a slow backend.
codes.fix: Optimize the slow backend, increase proxy timeout, or add caching.

codes.name: HTTP Version Not Supported
codes.summary: The HTTP version used in the request is not supported by the server.
codes.when: The client requested an HTTP version the server refuses.
codes.fix: Use a supported HTTP version (e.g. HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2).

codes.name: Network Authentication Required
codes.summary: The client needs to authenticate to gain network access.
codes.when: Used by captive portals (e.g. airport/hotel WiFi) to require login.
codes.fix: Complete the network login (captive portal) to gain access.
